name: Loading States & Perceived Performance
definition_origin: Jakob Nielsen's three response-time thresholds (0.1s instant, 1.0s flow-uninterrupted, 10s attention-loss) were published in Chapter 5 of 'Usability Engineering' (Academic Press, 1993) and have been republished in NN/g's 'Response Times: The 3 Important Limits' article (Jan 1, 1993, never edited substantively since). Nielsen built these on Robert B. Miller's 'Response Time in Man-Computer Conversational Transactions' (AFIPS Fall Joint Computer Conference, 1968) and Stuart Card's 'The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction' (1983). The term 'skeleton screen' was coined by Luke Wroblewski in his 2013 post 'Mobile Design Details: Avoid The Spinner' (lukew.com), based on his work designing the Polar mobile app. Optimistic UI as a named pattern was popularized by Apollo GraphQL docs (~2017) and Linear's engineering team (Tuomas Artman's 2020+ talks on the Linear Sync Engine). Material Design's motion guidance (m3.material.io/styles/motion) and Apple HIG's 'Loading' page formalize platform-level expectations.
core_mechanism: Perceived performance ≠ actual performance. Three psychological mechanisms operate: (1) Active vs. passive waiting — Maister's 1985 'Psychology of Waiting Lines' showed unoccupied time feels longer than occupied time; skeleton screens occupy attention by previewing structure, spinners do not. (2) Progress as goal-gradient — humans perceive movement-toward-goal as progress, so any incremental reveal (skeleton fade-in, percentage bar, content streaming) reduces perceived wait. (3) Optimistic-UI exploits the prediction-error system — by rendering the success state immediately, the brain registers task-complete dopamine before network latency arrives; if the prediction fails (server error), the user has had ~300-2000ms of relief that softens the rollback. Nielsen's thresholds map to neurological constants: 0.1s is below conscious-perception limit (the 'feels instant' window per Miller 1968 reflex-level), 1.0s is the limit of working-memory continuity (you forget what you were doing past this), 10s is the attention-span ceiling before dopamine-craving switches to another task. Optimistic UI is essentially a latency-hiding strategy — Linear uses it to make a sync-over-internet feel as instant as a local SQLite write.
neuroscience_correlate: Working-memory consolidation (prefrontal cortex + hippocampus) decays past ~1s without rehearsal — the 1-second threshold matches Cowan's (2001) 4±1 working-memory chunk limit. Anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) registers prediction-error when expected response doesn't arrive by ~250ms, generating a 'feedback-related negativity' (FRN) ERP signal — the neurological cost of laggy UI. Dopaminergic prediction-error: reward delivered earlier than expected produces a phasic dopamine burst (Schultz 1998), which is exactly what optimistic UI engineers — instant 'success' delivers the dopamine before the server actually confirms.
empirical_evidence: Skeleton-screen perception study (Bill Chung, Viget Labs / Facebook research, 2017, n≈100): users perceived skeleton-screen pages as ~20-30% faster to load than identical pages with spinners, despite identical actual load time. Bharadwaj et al., 'The effect of skeleton screens: Users' perception of speed and ease of navigation' (ResearchGate / ICUMT 2018, n=156): pages with skeleton screens scored statistically significantly higher on perceived speed (mean 6.2 vs. 4.8 on a 7-point Likert) and ease of navigation. Google Doodle progress-bar experiments (cited in Material guidelines): determinate progress indicators reduced abandonment ~22% vs. indeterminate spinners on operations >3s. Amazon's classic 100ms = 1% revenue loss study (2007, internal, reported by Greg Linden) — the foundational data point that hard latency materially harms business KPIs. Akamai 2017 mobile study: 53% of mobile users abandon a site that takes >3s to load. Linear's published engineering posts (Tuomas Artman, 2023) on the Linear Sync Engine cite p99 mutation latency of <100ms perceived (actual server roundtrip 80-300ms+) due to optimistic local-first writes via MobX. Nielsen's thresholds themselves are derived from Card 1991, Miller 1968, and have not been empirically refuted in 30+ years across hundreds of replications (NN/g 'The Need for Speed in AI', 2024).
measurable_outcome_metric: Time-to-Interactive (TTI), Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) <2.5s, First Input Delay (FID) <100ms, Interaction-to-Next-Paint (INP) <200ms (Chrome 2024 Core Web Vital), perceived-load-time on user-survey (5- or 7-point Likert), abandonment rate at 3s/5s/10s thresholds, optimistic-rollback rate (% of optimistic updates that fail and need rollback — should be <2% in production), spinner-display-time (a metric to MINIMIZE — every visible spinner-second is a UX cost), task-completion rate on slow-network conditions (3G throttle test).
engineering_tactics: RULE OF THUMB by Nielsen threshold: <100ms response → no UI feedback needed (just render). 100ms-1s → subtle hover/press state, no spinner (a spinner that flashes for 200ms is worse than nothing). 1-3s → skeleton screen or inline shimmer. >3s → determinate progress with percentage AND descriptive label ('Uploading 3 of 12 photos'). >10s → background-process with notification + ability to leave. SKELETON SCREENS — React Native: react-native-skeleton-placeholder or Moti's <Skeleton/>; web: react-loading-skeleton or Tailwind animate-pulse on bg-neutral-200/dark:bg-neutral-800 rounded blocks matching final-content geometry exactly (same width, height, border-radius). Match the layout 1:1 — a skeleton that does NOT match the post-load layout causes layout-shift (CLS hit). Use shimmer animation 1.2-1.6s loop, ease-in-out, opacity 0.6→1.0. OPTIMISTIC UI — React 19: const [optimisticTasks, addOptimisticTask] = useOptimistic(tasks, (state, newTask) => [...state, { ...newTask, pending: true }]); inside startTransition, addOptimisticTask then await server.create(); rollback automatic on error. TanStack Query: useMutation({ onMutate: async (newTask) => { await queryClient.cancelQueries(['tasks']); const previous = queryClient.getQueryData(['tasks']); queryClient.setQueryData(['tasks'], (old) => [...old, newTask]); return { previous }; }, onError: (_, __, ctx) => queryClient.setQueryData(['tasks'], ctx.previous), onSettled: () => queryClient.invalidateQueries(['tasks']) }). Show pending tasks with subtle visual difference (opacity 0.6 OR pending dot OR italic) so users know it's optimistic. PROGRESS — for finite operations, ALWAYS use determinate (<progress>, ProgressView, ProgressBar). For unknown duration, prefer skeleton over spinner. STREAMING — for AI/LLM responses, render token-by-token; do not buffer to completion. For images, use blur-up placeholder (next/image, expo-image's placeholder prop with thumbhash or blurhash). DEBOUNCE LOADING STATES — only show spinner if operation exceeds 200ms (otherwise it's flicker): const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false); useEffect(() => { const t = setTimeout(() => setLoading(true), 200); return () => clearTimeout(t); }, [trigger]). MINIMUM-DURATION pattern: once shown, keep spinner for at least 500ms to avoid flicker on fast networks. SUSPENSE — React Suspense + Server Components for built-in waterfall management; never show <Suspense fallback={<Spinner/>}/>, prefer skeleton. Offline-first / local-first (Linear, Figma, Notion) — write to local store first, sync in background; this is the ultimate optimistic UI.
real_world_examples: Linear (linear.app): keyboard-shortcut C creates issue, the issue appears in the list and detail-pane <16ms after keypress, mutation queued to backend; perceived latency near zero across continents. Tuomas Artman's React Helsinki talk documents the architecture (MobX object graph + transaction queue). Things 3 (Cultured Code): the Magic Plus Button drag-creates a task that appears instantly with a soft Taptic-Engine selection feedback at touch-up; sync to iCloud is silent and asynchronous. Apple Mail iOS: archive-swipe disappears the message immediately; the IMAP move happens in background, with an Undo toast for ~3s — classic optimistic pattern with safety net. Twitter/X: like-button heart fills instantly; counter increments instantly; rollback if API fails. Facebook (the original skeleton-screen popularizer 2014): News Feed with grey block placeholders matching post layout. LinkedIn feed: skeleton + shimmer matching card geometry. YouTube mobile: gray box with thumbnail crossfade-in. GitHub: 'Skeleton' loaders on Pulls/Issues lists since 2020. Slack: messages appear instantly on send (optimistic) with a tiny clock icon if not yet delivered, transitioning to checkmark on confirm. Notion: every block-edit is optimistic with last-write-wins reconciliation. Figma: every cursor move + property change is optimistic, multi-cursor sync is the proof that local-first scales. Discord: send-message is optimistic with a 'pending' opacity fade if the gateway is laggy. Apollo GraphQL docs popularized the pattern via the optimisticResponse field (~2017). Vercel/Next.js: route prefetch on link hover + Suspense streaming on navigation = sub-100ms perceived nav.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile is more latency-sensitive — touch input has no hover state to camouflage server roundtrip, and cellular networks vary (5G 30ms, weak LTE 800ms+). Mobile MUST default to optimistic + offline-first; the user's network is hostile. Web has hover-prefetch (link prefetch on mouseover, Vercel's <Link>), service-worker caching, and tighter assumptions about wired/wifi connectivity. Mobile UI also has zero-hover, so loading states must be distinguishable purely by tap-state. Mobile uses native skeleton libraries (Moti, react-native-skeleton-placeholder) and platform components (UIActivityIndicator, ProgressBar). Web has CSS-only shimmer (background: linear-gradient + animation) — cheap and dependency-free.
platform_design_language_alignment: iOS 26 Liquid Glass — uses translucent shimmer + glass-blur on loading states; Apple's HIG explicitly favors progress indicators over spinners for >3s ops. SwiftUI Suspense-equivalent is .task and .redacted(reason: .placeholder) — built-in skeleton. Material 3 Expressive (Android 16+) — spring-physics motion for skeleton-shimmer; Material guidelines now recommend skeleton over circular progress for content load. Both platforms align fully with the optimistic-UI + skeleton paradigm. Ship-faster: use platform-native skeleton (.redacted on iOS, Material's Loading API on Android) before reaching for third-party libs.
claude_design_application: Specify the threshold, the loading pattern, the actual asset to render, and the rollback strategy. Example prompt: 'Implement an optimistic-UI task-completion flow in React 19 + TanStack Query. On checkbox tap, immediately update local cache via queryClient.setQueryData (mark task.completed=true and add to today\u0027s completion list with pending:true). Fire mutation in parallel. On 200, set pending:false. On 4xx/5xx, rollback by setting cache to ctx.previous AND surface a toast: "Couldn\u0027t save. Retry?" with a Retry action. Do NOT show a spinner anywhere in this flow — the UI must feel instant. Pair the optimistic update with Haptics.notificationAsync(Success) at +0ms.' Second prompt: 'Build the feed loading state with a skeleton screen, not a spinner. Use Moti\u0027s Skeleton component. Generate 6 skeleton cards each matching the real card geometry: 64x64px avatar circle, 12px-tall title bar (75% width), 8px subtitle bar (45% width), 16px gap. Animate with shimmer 1.4s ease-in-out, opacity 0.6→1.0. Use design-tokens neutral.200 (light) / neutral.800 (dark) for the placeholder fill. Show skeleton only if data takes >200ms to arrive (debounce); minimum-display 500ms once shown.'
stitch_application: Stitch needs the loading state described as concrete visual prose, including the exact placeholder geometry, animation, and the trigger threshold. Example fragment: 'Feed loading state — show 6 skeleton cards stacked vertically with 16px gap. Each card: 360px wide, 88px tall, white background, 12px border-radius, 1px border in neutral-200. Inside each card: a 64x64px circle on the left (background neutral-200), then to its right two stacked horizontal bars — top bar 12px tall, 240px wide, neutral-200 fill, 4px corner radius; bottom bar 8px tall, 160px wide, same fill, 6px gap below the top bar. ANIMATION INTENT (devs implement): horizontal shimmer gradient sweeping left-to-right across each placeholder, 1.4s duration, ease-in-out, infinite; opacity 0.6 to 1.0 to 0.6. Show this state only when network response exceeds 200ms; once shown, hold for at least 500ms to avoid flicker on fast networks. After data arrives, fade the skeletons out over 200ms while real cards crossfade in.' Second fragment: 'Task-completion optimistic flow — when user taps the empty circle next to a task, the circle fills with the brand green (#22C55E) and a checkmark draws in over 240ms (path stroke-dashoffset animation). The task title strikes through and dims to 50% opacity over 320ms. SAVING-INDICATOR INTENT: a 4x4px gray dot appears 12px to the right of the task title for the duration of the network call (typically 80-400ms). On error, dot turns red (#EF4444), a snackbar slides up from the bottom: "Couldn\u0027t save. Tap to retry." Devs: implement via TanStack Query optimistic mutation with onError rollback.'
ai_codegen_failure_mode: (1) AI generates a centered <Spinner/> for every loading state, including 200ms operations → flicker city. Counter: 'Use skeleton screens for content lists. Reserve spinners for <button>-internal pending states only. Debounce loading state by 200ms before showing.' (2) AI uses indeterminate spinner for known-duration ops (file uploads). Counter: 'Use determinate <progress> with percentage AND label whenever total is known.' (3) AI puts <Suspense fallback={<Spinner/>}/> wrappers everywhere. Counter: 'Suspense fallbacks must be skeleton screens matching the route geometry, not spinners.' (4) AI does not implement optimistic UI by default — every mutation has a loading state. Counter: 'Use TanStack Query useMutation with onMutate/onError for optimistic updates on all idempotent and low-stakes operations (likes, completions, edits, sends). Server reconciliation is the source of truth, but UI must update before the network call.' (5) AI omits debouncing and minimum-display logic → spinner flickers on fast networks. Counter: 'Debounce 200ms before showing loading; once shown, hold for ≥500ms.' (6) AI uses a generic skeleton (gray box) that doesn\u0027t match real layout → CLS jump on load. Counter: 'Skeleton geometry must match final-content geometry exactly: same width/height/border-radius/avatar-size.' (7) AI forgets the rollback path — happy-path optimistic only. Counter: 'On error, snap state back to pre-mutation snapshot AND show a recoverable toast with retry action.' (8) AI animates skeleton shimmer at 60Hz CPU cost on RN — no native driver. Counter: 'Use useNativeDriver: true on the shimmer animation, or use Moti/Reanimated for layout-thread shimmer.' (9) AI shows long-running operations as a single 'Loading...' string. Counter: 'For ops >3s, show step-labels: "Uploading photos (3 of 12)" / "Generating preview" / "Almost there".'
anti_patterns: Spinners on operations <500ms (causes flicker — feels broken). Indeterminate spinners on operations with known duration (a percentage would do). Full-screen spinner that blocks the entire UI for any backend call (hostage-taking UI). Skeleton screens that don\u0027t match the final layout (CLS hit, jarring jump). Optimistic UI without rollback path (silent data loss). Optimistic UI on irreversible/destructive actions (delete account should NEVER optimistically remove account). Loading toast on every fetch (notification spam). Spinning logo as 'branded loading' — Apple-pinwheel-of-death vibes. Hard-blocking modal during data sync. AI-slop variant: <div className='animate-spin'/> centered on every page that ever fetches data.
regulatory_risk: Low. WCAG 2.2.1 (Timing Adjustable) requires user control if process times out. WCAG 2.2.2 (Pause/Stop/Hide) — animations >5s must be pausable, which affects shimmer loops technically but rarely flagged. CSP/network failure handling has emerging EU NIS2/CRA implications for resilient-by-default. Optimistic UI on financial transactions can mislead users about transaction status — FCA/SEC have guidance against showing 'completed' before settlement (Robinhood-style 'instant' framing has drawn scrutiny). Otherwise legally benign.
pitfalls_caveats: Optimistic-UI rollback UX is the hardest part — getting users to believe a state did NOT happen after they saw it happen requires careful copy and visual choreography. Skeleton screens cost engineering time (every list needs a hand-tuned skeleton) — easy to skip on internal admin pages where it doesn\u0027t matter. INP (Chrome's new Core Web Vital, 2024) penalizes long-tasks-to-paint — heavy skeleton renders can themselves hurt INP. Animations cost CPU/battery on low-end Android — keep shimmer infrequent and prefer GPU-accelerated transforms. Accessibility: aria-live='polite' for loading-state announcements (don\u0027t spam screen readers); aria-busy='true' on the skeleton container; honor prefers-reduced-motion to disable shimmer. Optimistic UI breaks audit trails if you forget to log the actual server confirmation separately from the optimistic event.
cross_cultural_validity: Nielsen's thresholds are biologically grounded (auditory/visual perception, working memory) and are universal across cultures. Network conditions vary massively: median 4G in India, parts of Africa and SE Asia is 1.5-3 Mbps, so optimistic + offline-first is even MORE critical in those markets — Facebook Lite, Instagram Lite, YouTube Go are case studies. Patience norms differ subtly: a 2014 Akamai study found APAC users abandon at slightly lower thresholds (~2.5s) than NA/EU users (~3s) but this is a small effect. RTL (Arabic/Hebrew) requires shimmer animation to flip direction (right-to-left sweep). Skeleton-screen geometry must adapt to RTL layouts (margins, avatar position). No evidence of culturally-specific skeleton or spinner conventions.
sources: [Jakob Nielsen — Response Times: The 3 Important Limits (NN/g)](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/response-times-3-important-limits/)
[Luke Wroblewski — Mobile Design Details: Avoid The Spinner (2013)](https://www.lukew.com/ff/entry.asp?1797=)

[Bharadwaj et al. — The effect of skeleton screens: Users' perception of speed and ease of navigation](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/326858669_The_effect_of_skeleton_screens_Users'_perception_of_speed_and_ease_of_navigation)
[Tuomas Artman — Building a synchronous experience with asynchronous data (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bnOpm3a1fRE)
[Chrome — Interaction to Next Paint (INP) Core Web Vital](https://web.dev/articles/inp)
